title: Things to Never Skip on a Cheap Car

content:
## Things to Never Skip on a Cheap Car

Let's face it, finding a good, affordable car can feel like striking gold. The allure of a low price tag is powerful, but it's easy to get carried away and overlook potential pitfalls. A cheap car doesn't have to mean a bad car, but it does mean you need to be extra vigilant. At BuyAndScrap.com, we believe everyone deserves a reliable set of wheels, no matter their budget. That's why we're sharing the essential **things to never skip on a cheap car** inspection.

Cutting corners now can lead to much bigger bills later, so grab a cuppa, take a deep breath, and let's make sure your next budget motor is a brilliant one!

### Before You Even See the Car

Before you've even laid eyes on the vehicle, there's crucial detective work to do. This preliminary research is one of the most important **things to never skip on a cheap car** hunt.

#### Do Your Homework

*   **MOT History Check:** Head over to the Gov.uk website and input the car's registration number. You'll get its full MOT history, including advisories and failure reasons. Look for recurring issues, high repair costs, or significant gaps in testing. This can tell you a lot about how well the car has been maintained.
*   **Vehicle History Check (HPI Check):** Don't skimp on this! For a small fee, an HPI check (or similar service) will tell you if the car has outstanding finance, has been written off, stolen, or has a dodgy mileage reading. This is an absolute non-negotiable step.
*   **Research Common Faults:** A quick Google search for the specific make and model you're looking at can reveal common problems or recalls. Knowing these beforehand helps you know what to specifically look out for when you view the car.

### The Physical Inspection: Getting Your Hands Dirty (A Little!)

Once you're face-to-face with the car, it's time for a thorough visual inspection. Think of it as dating – you're looking for red flags!

#### Exterior & Under the Bonnet

*   **Rust:** Pay close attention to wheel arches, sills, boot lid, and around the windows. Surface rust isn't ideal but deep, bubbling rust is a major warning sign. Also, peek underneath for rust on suspension components and the exhaust.
*   **Panel Gaps:** Are the gaps between panels (bonnet, doors, boot) even? Uneven gaps can indicate poor quality accident repair.
*   **Tyres:** Check the tread depth (should be above the legal minimum of 1.6mm across 75% of the tyre's width) and look for cracks or bulges. Uneven wear could point to suspension or alignment issues. Also, check if all tyres are the same brand – mismatched tyres can sometimes indicate cost-cutting.
*   **Fluids:** Ask the seller to pop the bonnet. Check the engine oil, coolant, and brake fluid levels. Look for any strange colours (e.g., milky oil, rusty coolant) or leaks underneath the car.
*   **Belts & Hoses:** Look for cracks or fraying on drive belts and hoses. These can be expensive to replace if they fail.

#### Interior Checks

*   **Warning Lights:** When you turn the ignition key, all warning lights should illuminate and then go out (except handbrake/seatbelt). If a light stays on or doesn't appear at all, it's a concern.
*   **Electrics:** Test everything – windows, central locking, radio, air conditioning, wipers, lights. A faulty electric window can be surprisingly costly to fix.
*   **Dampness & Smells:** Check under the mats for dampness, and sniff for any musty odours. This could indicate leaks or a previous flood. A strong air freshener might be trying to hide something.

### The All-Important Test Drive

This is where the car needs to prove itself on the road. A test drive is one of the most critical **things to never skip on a cheap car** purchase.

#### Listen, Feel, Observe

*   **Start-up:** Ideally, start the car from cold. Listen for any difficulty starting, smoke from the exhaust, or unusual noises.
*   **Engine & Transmission:** Listen for knocking, rattling, or whining noises. Does the car accelerate smoothly? Do the gears change easily and without crunching or hesitation? For automatics, check for jerky changes.
*   **Steering & Brakes:** Does the steering feel loose or does the car pull to one side? Do the brakes feel spongy or make grinding noises? Test them safely at different speeds. The car should stop in a straight line without excessive nose-dive.
*   **Suspension:** Drive over some bumps or uneven roads (safely, of course). Listen for clonks or rattles, which could indicate worn suspension components.

### Paperwork & Legalities

Even if the car seems perfect, the final hurdle is the paperwork. Verifying paperwork is another one of those **things to never skip on a cheap car** purchase.

#### Verify Everything

*   **V5C Logbook:** Ensure the V5C (logbook) is present, in the seller's name and address, and that the VIN (Vehicle Identification Number) matches the one on the car's chassis and windscreen.
*   **Service History:** A full service history with stamps and receipts is a massive bonus, especially for a cheap car. It shows the car has been looked after.
*   **MOT Certificate:** Even though it's online, having the physical certificate (or a printout) is good. Check the mileage on the certificate matches the car's odometer.

### Don't Be Afraid to Walk Away

Your gut feeling matters. If something doesn't feel right, or the seller seems evasive, be prepared to walk away. There are always other cars out there, and your peace of mind (and wallet) is worth more than a potentially problematic bargain.

Finding a budget car that's reliable is absolutely achievable with a bit of patience and thoroughness. By remembering these essential checks, you'll be well on your way to a smart purchase at BuyAndScrap.com.
